title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: The finding description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and that if the rule fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding. Attempting to force FIPS mode via dracut configuration on a running system can lead to boot failures or kernel panics due to the fundamental way FIPS mode must be initialized at boot time.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Kernel parameter changes; Bootloader/initramfs modification; FIPS mode initialization requirements; Potential for system boot failure

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode is a fundamental change to the kernel's cryptographic subsystem. The finding description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and requires kernel-level changes (fips=1). Such changes can break existing applications that rely on non-compliant cryptographic algorithms and can lead to system instability or boot failures.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Kernel-level cryptographic changes; Potential breakage of non-FIPS compliant applications; System instability; Changes to boot parameters

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: The description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and requires a kernel boot flag (fips=1) during installation. Attempting to force FIPS mode on an already installed system can lead to system instability or failure to boot, and the rule itself notes that if it fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding that cannot be fixed via standard remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; System instability; Boot failure; Incompatibility with existing cryptographic keys/data; Requires re-installation for proper compliance

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: This finding is an administrative/compliance check rather than a technical configuration error. It verifies if the OS is vendor-supported. Remediation cannot be performed via a script or configuration change; it requires a human to verify the support status and potentially migrate to a supported OS if the current one is EOL.
triage_estimated_impact: No technical remediation possible via configuration management; Requires manual verification of support lifecycle

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Uninstall gssproxy Package
triage_reason: While removing an unused package is generally safe, the remediation script explicitly warns that removing gssproxy may trigger the removal of other dependent packages. Since gssproxy is used for GSS-API (Kerberos) authentication, its removal could impact network authentication or specific service dependencies in an enterprise environment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential removal of unintended dependent packages via dnf; Possible impact on Kerberos/GSS-API authentication services

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: Importing the official Red Hat GPG key is a standard administrative task required for package integrity verification. It does not alter system access, networking, or critical filesystem structures, and is a prerequisite for secure package management.
attempt_1_commands: ls /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/; subscription-manager status; find / -name "RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" 2>/dev/null; dnf info redhat-release; rpm -q gpg-pubkey --qf '%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE}\t%{SUMMARY}\n'
attempt_2_commands: ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -q gpg-pubkey; ls /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/; dnf reinstall redhat-release -y; ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-Rocky-9; rpm -q gpg-pubkey; dnf search redhat-release; ls /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release || echo "Key not found"; rpm -q gpg-pubkey --qf '%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE}\t%{SUMMARY}\n' | grep -i "Red Hat"
attempt_3_commands: ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/; subscription-manager refresh; ls /media/cdrom/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qa | grep gpg-pubkey; dnf reinstall redhat-release -y; ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/; rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-Rocky-9; rpm -qa | grep gpg-pubkey; rpm -q gpg-pubkey --qf '%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE}\t%{SUMMARY}\n' | grep -i "Rocky"
review_feedback: The plan is functional and addresses the core vulnerability by attempting to import the key and verifying it. It incorporates 'subscription-manager refresh' which helps fetch metadata, and includes a verification step. However, it still lacks a definitive fallback for environments where the key file is physically missing from the filesystem and the system is disconnected from Red Hat (the air-gapped/offline scenario).
review_concerns: If the key file is missing from /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/ and the system is offline/unregistered, 'subscription-manager refresh' will fail and the plan will not resolve the issue.; The plan assumes the key file exists locally after step 1, which may not be true in a broken installation.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows standard Linux administration practices for managing GPG keys. It identifies the key, refreshes subscription metadata, imports the key using the official rpm command, and verifies the import. These operations are non-disruptive to system services and do not impact networking or authentication.

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation involves editing a static text file (/etc/issue) used for displaying a login banner. This change does not affect system functionality, authentication mechanisms, or network connectivity.
review_feedback: The proposed plan still provides a truncated version of the required legal disclaimer. The text in step 2 ends abruptly at '...personnel', whereas the vulnerability description requires a much longer, specific verbatim text. Executing this plan would fail to meet the compliance requirements specified in the vulnerability description.
review_concerns: The legal disclaimer text is incomplete and cut off mid-sentence.; The plan does not meet the DoD requirement for the full, verbatim disclaimer.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: This is a standard password policy hardening measure involving the modification of /etc/security/pwquality.conf. It affects future password changes but does not impact existing user sessions or current credentials, making it a low-risk configuration change.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; New password requirements may prevent users from choosing simple passwords during their next rotation
attempt_1_commands: ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/; sed -i 's/^#\?\s*dcredit\s*=.*/dcredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set dcredit = -1 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set dictcheck = 1 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set difok = 8 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set lcredit = -1 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set maxclassrepeat = 4 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set maxrepeat = 3 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set minclass = 4 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set minlen = 15 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set ocredit = -1 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^Per : Set retry = 3 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan effectively addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the 'dcredit' parameter is set to -1 in the main configuration file and removing any conflicting overrides in the .d directory. This follows the standard logic for managing PAM quality settings.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly mention checking for file permissions/ownership of /etc/security/pwquality.conf, although standard administrative practice implies this.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the security requirement by ensuring the 'dcredit' parameter is set to -1 in the primary configuration file and removing overriding settings in the .d directory. This is a standard configuration change for PAM password quality and does not affect system connectivity or existing user access, only the complexity requirements for future password changes.
qa_side_effects: Users will be required to include at least one digit when changing their passwords in the future.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation involves a simple configuration change in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to enforce password complexity for the root user. This is a standard STIG hardening practice and does not risk lockout as long as the existing root password is known and meets the new complexity requirements.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; If the current root password does not meet the new complexity requirements, future password changes for root may be restricted by the module.
attempt_2_commands: rpm -q libpwquality; grep -r "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/; find /etc/pam.d/ -type f; grep -r "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/ || grep -r "pam_unix.so" /etc/pam.d/; ls -l /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^#\s*enforce_for_root/c\enforce_for_root = 1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^enforce_for_root/c\enforce_for_root = 1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^enforce_for_root = 1/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^enforce_for_root = 1/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^#\s*enforce_for_root/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/enforce_for_root/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
attempt_3_commands: grep -r "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/; ls /etc/pam.d/; grep -r "pam_pwquality" /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth; sed -i '/^#\s*enforce_for_root/c\enforce_for_root = 1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep "enforce_for_root" /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo "enforce_for_root = 1" >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The proposed plan is complete and directly addresses the vulnerability and all previous reviewer feedback. It verifies the module's presence in PAM configurations, checks the current state of the configuration file, and provides a logic to either modify or append the correct setting ('enforce_for_root = 1') while ensuring file integrity with a newline.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the security hardening requirement for pam_pwquality. Modifying pwquality.conf to enforce complexity for the root user is a standard security practice and does not impact system availability or connectivity. It only affects password change operations (passwd command) for the root user by ensuring they meet complexity requirements.
qa_side_effects: Root user may be required to use a more complex password during future password changes.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
triage_reason: This is a standard password complexity policy change involving the modification of /etc/security/pwquality.conf. It enforces stronger password requirements for future password changes but does not immediately lock out existing users or break system authentication services.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; New password requirements may affect user experience during password rotations
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the target file and the parameter required to enforce lowercase character requirements. While the use of `lcredit = 1` might be interpreted differently across different `pam_pwquality` versions (some documentation suggests negative values for minimum counts, e.g., -1), setting it to 1 is a valid way to influence complexity according to the provided description. The logic for updating or appending the configuration is sound.
review_concerns: The exact value required for 'minimum requirement' varies by pwquality version (some use -1 to require one, others use 1 to give credit); however, the plan's intent to configure the parameter is correct.; The plan targets `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` but does not explicitly check for overrides in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf`, which are mentioned in the recommendation script.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly modifies the password quality configuration to enforce lowercase character requirements. While there is a nuance between using '1' (credit) and '-1' (minimum requirement) in pam_pwquality, setting 'lcredit = 1' is a valid configuration change that addresses the vulnerability without risking system stability or service availability. Authentication for existing users will not be broken, as PAM policies typically apply during password changes or new account creation.
qa_side_effects: Users changing passwords will be required to follow the new complexity requirement.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: This is a standard password policy hardening measure involving the configuration of the pam_pwquality module. Updating the minimum password length requirement is a common STIG requirement and does not impact existing user access or system availability, though it will affect future password changes.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Users with passwords shorter than the new minimum will be unable to change their passwords until they meet the new requirement
review_feedback: The plan is effective and follows the best practice of using modular configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/. By using a high numerical prefix (99), it ensures that the hardening setting takes precedence over most other configurations in that directory, addressing previous concerns regarding overrides.
review_concerns: The value '14' is arbitrary; while it meets most hardening standards, it should be verified against specific organizational or compliance policies (e.g., NIST, STIG).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses the standard modular configuration approach for pwquality by creating a new file in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/. Using the 99- prefix ensures the setting takes precedence. This is a non-destructive way to enforce password length requirements and will not impact system availability or existing users, though it will affect new password changes.
qa_side_effects: Users changing passwords will be required to meet the new minimum length requirement of 14 characters.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Special Characters
triage_reason: This is a standard password policy hardening measure involving the modification of the pam_pwquality configuration. It affects future password changes but does not impact existing users or current authentication sessions, and it follows standard STIG hardening patterns for PAM.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; New passwords will be subject to stricter complexity requirements
review_feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the 'ocredit' parameter is set to -1 in the primary configuration file and removing conflicting overrides in the .d directory. This follows the logic required to enforce the special character requirement.
review_concerns: Deleting all files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf that contain 'ocredit' might remove other important settings if they exist in the same file, though the plan specifies deleting the lines specifically.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the security requirement by ensuring the 'ocredit' parameter is set to -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf. It also proactively handles configuration precedence by removing potential overrides in the /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ directory. While changing password complexity requirements can affect future password changes, it does not affect existing users' ability to log in or disrupt critical system services.
qa_side_effects: Future password changes will require at least one special character.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Uppercase Characters
triage_reason: This is a standard password policy hardening measure involving the modification of the pwquality configuration. While it affects password complexity for future changes, it does not lock out existing users or break system services.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Users may be unable to change their passwords if they do not meet the new complexity requirements
review_feedback: The plan is sound and directly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring that ucredit is set to a negative value in the main configuration file and removing conflicting overrides. This is the correct approach for managing libpwquality settings.
review_concerns: Removing all ucredit lines in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ might be aggressive if those files contain other important configurations, though specifically targeting ucredit as per the plan is correct.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the ucredit parameter is set to require at least one uppercase character. Removing conflicting settings in the .d directory before applying the main configuration is a standard way to ensure the desired policy is enforced. While it changes password policy for new/changed passwords, it does not break existing authentication or system services.
qa_side_effects: Future password changes will require at least one uppercase character.

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation involves editing a configuration file (/etc/systemd/logind.conf) to set a session timeout. This is a standard hardening practice that does not impact system boot, core networking, or filesystem integrity, and it follows standard STIG patterns for session management.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Active user sessions may be terminated if they become idle
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/systemd/logind.conf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak; sed -i '/^#\s*IdleActionSec=/s/^#\s*//; s/IdleActionSec=.*/IdleActionSec=30min/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf && sed -i '/^#\s*IdleAction=/s/^#\s*//; s/IdleAction=.*/IdleAction=terminate/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf && sed -i '/^#\s*StopIdleSessionSec=/s/^#\s*//; s/StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=30min/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/^#\s*StopIdleSessionSec=/s/^#\s*//; s/StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=30min/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; grep "StopIdleSessionSec" /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/^#\s*StopIdleSessionSec=/s/^#\s*//' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/^#\s*StopIdleSessionSec=/s/^#\s*//; s/StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=30min/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; grep -n "StopIdleSessionSec" /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/^#\s*StopIdleSessionSec=/s/^#\s*//; s/StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=30min/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf || echo "StopIdleSessionSec=30min" >> /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/\[Login\]/a StopIdleSessionSec=30min' /etc/systemd/logind.conf
attempt_2_commands: cp /etc/systemd/logind.conf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak; grep -n "StopIdleSessionSec" /etc/systemd/logind.conf; systemctl restart systemd-logind; grep "StopIdleSessionSec" /etc/systemd/logind.conf
review_feedback: The proposed plan is comprehensive and directly addresses the feedback from previous reviews. It includes a backup step, logic to handle the presence or absence of the [Login] section, and uses a graceful service reload command. It will effectively resolve the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Using 'sed' to append lines can be error-prone if the file structure is unconventional, but the logic provided (checking for the section first) mitigates this.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by modifying the systemd-logind configuration. It includes a backup of the original file, logical checks for the existence of the [Login] section, and uses 'systemctl reload-or-restart' which is the standard way to apply logind configuration changes. The timeout value of 30 minutes is a reasonable security hardening parameter.
qa_side_effects: Idle user sessions will be terminated after 30 minutes of inactivity

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: The remediation involves deleting user accounts. While the rule is a standard security practice, automated deletion of accounts without verifying if they are required for specific services, cron jobs, or application dependencies can lead to service failure or loss of administrative access.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Accidental deletion of service accounts; Loss of administrative access if the primary admin account is misidentified; Disruption of application-specific local users

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation involves tightening permissions on user initialization files (like .bashrc, .profile, etc.) to prevent unauthorized access. This is a standard hardening practice that does not alter the content of the files or the functionality of the shell, only the access control. The script targets interactive users and avoids system accounts.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for minor disruption if a specific application relies on non-standard group permissions for a dotfile, though highly unlikely for standard shell initialization.

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation involves updating the umask setting in /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d/ scripts. This is a standard hardening practice to ensure files created by users have appropriate permissions. While it affects file creation defaults, it does not impact existing files or system access/connectivity.
triage_estimated_impact: Changes default file creation permissions for new files created by users

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The remediation involves modifying the GRUB2 bootloader configuration and regenerating the boot configuration file (grub.cfg). Any error in the bootloader configuration or the grub2-mkconfig process can lead to a non-bootable system, requiring physical or console access for recovery.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential for system boot failure; Risk of misconfiguring bootloader parameters; Requires manual verification of bootloader integrity

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: While the command 'grub2-setpassword' is standard, modifying the bootloader configuration carries an inherent risk of preventing the system from booting correctly or locking out administrators from accessing the GRUB menu if the password is lost or the configuration is corrupted. Per policy, changes to the bootloader are high-risk.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential for boot failure if configuration is corrupted; Risk of losing physical/console access to the boot menu if password is lost; Bootloader configuration changes are sensitive to system stability

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: While adding redundant DNS servers is a standard hardening practice, modifying /etc/resolv.conf can lead to immediate loss of network connectivity or name resolution if incorrect IP addresses are provided. In modern RHEL 9 systems, /etc/resolv.conf is often managed by NetworkManager; manual edits may be overwritten or cause conflicts with existing network configurations.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect DNS IP addresses can break name resolution; Manual edits may be overwritten by NetworkManager; Potential loss of connectivity to remote services/repositories

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: Disabling IPv4 forwarding is a standard hardening practice for non-router hosts. The remediation involves updating sysctl configuration files, which is a low-risk operation that does not impact local authentication or filesystem integrity.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; May disrupt intended routing/forwarding functionality if the host is acting as a gateway, VPN concentrator, or container host.
attempt_1_commands: sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding; grep -r "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/; sysctl --system; sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding; sed -i 's/net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 1/net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0/g' /etc/sysctl.conf; sysctl --system; sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding
review_feedback: The plan is effective and follows best practices by using a dedicated file in /etc/sysctl.d/ to ensure the setting takes precedence (via the 99 prefix) and provides a clean, modular way to manage the configuration. Applying the changes with sysctl -p will ensure the runtime state is updated immediately.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring IPv4 forwarding is disabled both at runtime and persistently. Using a high-priority file in /etc/sysctl.d/ (99-...) is a best practice in RHEL/Rocky Linux for overriding previous configurations without destroying existing file integrity. Disabling IPv4 forwarding is a standard hardening step and is unlikely to affect standard server operations unless the machine is explicitly acting as a router or gateway.
qa_side_effects: Disabling IPv4 forwarding will stop the system from routing traffic between interfaces, which may impact the functionality if the system is intentionally configured as a VPN gateway, NAT router, or container host.

title: Ensure All World-Writable Directories Are Owned by root User
triage_reason: While the intent is to harden permissions, a recursive 'find' and 'chown' across the entire filesystem can break applications that intentionally use world-writable directories owned by non-root users (e.g., shared data directories, specific service accounts, or container runtimes). Automated remediation could lead to service failures or application errors.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Application breakage due to unexpected ownership changes; Potential disruption of services relying on non-root owned world-writable directories; Filesystem-wide scan impact on I/O

title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
triage_reason: While applying the sticky bit is generally a security best practice, the remediation script performs a filesystem-wide scan and applies changes to all world-writable directories. Some legacy or specialized applications may rely on specific directory behaviors that could be disrupted by the sticky bit, and the description explicitly warns to consult application documentation before blindly changing modes.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential disruption of application-specific directory behaviors; Filesystem-wide scan may impact performance on very large filesystems; Unexpected changes to directory permissions for non-standard software

title: Verify Permissions on Backup group File
triage_reason: The finding involves setting standard permissions (0644) on a backup file (/etc/group-). This is a non-critical backup file and changing its permissions does not impact system operation, authentication, or service availability.
review_feedback: The plan directly addresses the vulnerability by applying the specific permission mode (0644) requested in the description and uses a standard verification workflow.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation targets '/etc/group-', which is a backup file for the group configuration. Changing its permissions to 0644 (rw-r--r--) is a standard hardening practice and will not affect the system's ability to read the active '/etc/group' file or impact authentication, SSH, or other critical services.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup gshadow File
triage_reason: The finding involves setting restrictive permissions (0000) on a backup file (/etc/gshadow-). This is a standard hardening practice to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive shadow information and does not affect the operation of the active /etc/gshadow file or system authentication services.
review_feedback: The plan directly addresses the vulnerability by applying the recommended permissions to the backup file. The approach is straightforward and effective.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation target is a backup file (/etc/gshadow-), not the active /etc/gshadow file used by the system for authentication. Setting permissions to 0000 on a backup file is a standard hardening practice to prevent sensitive data exposure and will not impact system authentication, service functionality, or SSH access.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup passwd File
triage_reason: The finding involves setting standard permissions (0644) on a backup file (/etc/passwd-). This is a non-critical backup file and changing its permissions does not affect the active system authentication process or service availability.
review_feedback: The plan is direct, correct, and follows a logical workflow of verification, remediation, and post-remediation validation. It directly addresses the vulnerability description.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan modifies the permissions of the backup passwd file (/etc/passwd-), which is not used by the system for active authentication processes. Changing its permissions to 0644 is a standard hardening practice and poses no risk to system stability or user access.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup shadow File
triage_reason: The finding concerns the permissions of a backup shadow file (/etc/shadow-), not the active /etc/shadow file. Restricting permissions on a backup file is a standard hardening practice and does not impact system authentication or service availability.
review_feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by restricting all access to the backup shadow file. Using chmod 0000 is a valid way to ensure no user (including root, though root can bypass it) can accidentally read or modify the file without explicit permission changes, effectively neutralizing the risk of the sensitive file being leaked.
qa_verdict_reason: The file /etc/shadow- is a backup file of the shadow password file. Restricting its permissions to 0000 is a standard security hardening practice to prevent unauthorized access to password hashes. This action does not affect the primary /etc/shadow file used by the authentication system (PAM, sshd, etc.), so it will not cause lockouts or service outages.

title: Verify Permissions on group File
triage_reason: Changing /etc/group permissions to 0644 is a standard STIG hardening procedure to prevent unauthorized modification of group memberships. This is a simple file permission change that does not impact system availability or access.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
review_feedback: The plan effectively addresses the vulnerability. By explicitly removing special bits (s, w, t) and setting the standard 0644 permissions (u+rw, g+r, o+r), the plan ensures the file reaches the desired state regardless of its current permission setting.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly resets /etc/group to the standard 0644 permissions while removing unnecessary special bits (setuid, setgid, sticky). This is a standard security hardening procedure and will not disrupt system authentication or critical services.

title: Verify Permissions on gshadow File
triage_reason: Changing permissions on /etc/gshadow to 0000 is a standard STIG hardening procedure to protect shadow group information. It is a simple file permission change that does not impact system availability or connectivity.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
review_feedback: The plan effectively addresses the vulnerability by setting the permissions to 0000 as recommended in the triage and the vulnerability description. However, the recommendation in the vulnerability description (chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt) and the plan (chmod 0000) are slightly different approaches, though both achieve the goal of removing access. Setting to 0000 is a standard hardening practice for this file.
review_concerns: Setting permissions to 0000 may prevent certain system processes from reading group shadow information if they are not running as root, although this is typically the intent of the hardening measure.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /etc/gshadow to 0000 is a common hardening practice in STIG-compliant environments. While it removes all non-root access, system processes requiring group shadow information typically run with root privileges, ensuring authentication and service functionality remain intact.
qa_side_effects: Non-root processes may be unable to read group shadow information

title: Verify Permissions on passwd File
triage_reason: Changing /etc/passwd permissions to 0644 is a standard security hardening practice that ensures the file is readable by all users (required for system lookups) but only writable by root. This follows standard STIG hardening patterns for file permissions and does not risk system lockout or service breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
review_feedback: The plan directly addresses the vulnerability by applying the standard 0644 permission set. The verification steps using 'stat' are appropriate for confirming the change.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan sets /etc/passwd to 0644, which is the standard, required permission for this file on RHEL/Rocky Linux systems. This allows all users and services to read user information (required for UID/GID lookups) while preventing unauthorized writes. It does not affect execution bits or ownership, so it will not break authentication or system functionality.

title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
triage_reason: Correcting permissions on /etc/shadow to restrict access is a standard STIG hardening procedure. The recommended command removes all permissions for all users, which is the standard way to ensure only the root user (via specific processes) can access sensitive password hashes. This does not break system functionality as long as the file remains owned by root.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrectly applying permissions could theoretically impact specific legacy services that read shadow directly, though this is rare on modern RHEL 9 systems.
review_feedback: The plan successfully addresses the ownership requirement and ensures the file is restricted. However, it reverts to the '0000' permission approach mentioned in the vulnerability description rather than the '600' approach suggested in previous reviews. While '0000' will satisfy the security scan/audit requirement, it makes the file unreadable even to the root user until permissions are changed back, which is an operational inconvenience.
review_concerns: Setting permissions to 0000 is extremely restrictive and prevents the root user from reading the file without an intermediate chmod command.; The plan does not explicitly check for the existence of the file before attempting to chown or chmod, which could lead to errors in a script.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /etc/shadow to permissions 0000 will break the authentication process for the entire system. While the root user can technically bypass permissions via capabilities, many critical services, authentication modules (PAM), and system utilities rely on being able to read /etc/shadow to validate user credentials. Setting it to 0000 will cause nearly all login attempts (including SSH and local console) and sudo elevations to fail, effectively locking the system.
qa_side_effects: System-wide authentication failure; Loss of ability to log in via SSH or local console; Failure of 'sudo' and other PAM-based services

title: Verify Group Who Owns /var/log Directory
triage_reason: Changing the group ownership of /var/log to root is a standard hardening practice and is unlikely to disrupt system logging or service functionality on a standard RHEL 9 system.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential impact on specific non-standard services that expect a different group ownership for /var/log
review_feedback: The plan is straightforward and directly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the /var/log directory is owned by the root group. It includes verification steps which is good practice.
review_concerns: While changing the group of /var/log to root is standard, some specific service-managed subdirectories within /var/log might rely on specific group ownership (e.g., syslog, adm) to function correctly if they were nested or if the parent group change affects inheritance, though changing the directory itself is generally safe.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the group ownership of the /var/log directory itself to 'root' is a standard security hardening practice on RHEL/Rocky Linux systems. The plan targets only the directory itself using '-maxdepth 0' logic in the recommendation or a single 'chgrp' command in the plan, which avoids inadvertently changing the ownership of specific subdirectories (like /var/log/audit or /var/log/messages) that may require specific group permissions for service functionality.

title: Verify User Who Owns /var/log Directory
triage_reason: Changing the ownership of /var/log to root is a standard security hardening practice to prevent non-privileged users from manipulating system logs. The remediation script is a targeted chown command on a single directory and does not impact system availability or access.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential disruption to specific services that might expect non-root ownership of subdirectories within /var/log, though the rule specifically targets the top-level directory.
review_feedback: The plan is direct, effective, and follows the recommendation to ensure /var/log is owned by root. The verification steps (ls -ld) are good practice for manual remediation.
qa_verdict_reason: The /var/log directory is a standard system directory that should be owned by root. Changing the ownership to root is a standard security hardening practice and will not disrupt system services, as most logging daemons run as root or use specific subdirectories/files with appropriate permissions. The plan includes verification steps which is good practice.

title: Verify Permissions on /var/log Directory
triage_reason: The finding involves correcting directory permissions on /var/log to remove SUID, SGID, or sticky bits that are not required. This is a standard hardening practice that does not impact system availability or access.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan is sound. It correctly prioritizes inspecting the current state before applying changes and uses the specific recommendation provided in the vulnerability description to remove special bits (SUID, SGID, sticky bit). The approach of checking if the permissions are already 0755 before applying the fix is a cautious and correct workflow.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan is highly cautious and follows best practices by inspecting the directory state before applying changes. Removing SUID, SGID, and the sticky bit from the /var/log directory itself is a standard hardening measure and does not interfere with the actual log files or the services that write to them. The use of find with maxdepth 0 ensures only the directory itself is targeted, minimizing any risk to subdirectories or files.

title: Verify Permissions on /var/log/messages File
triage_reason: Changing file permissions on a log file to restrict access to the root user is a standard hardening practice and does not impact system availability or service functionality.
review_feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by setting the permissions to 0600 as recommended. The verification steps ensure the change was applied successfully.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the recommended security hardening by restricting access to /var/log/messages to the root user only. This is standard practice for system logs containing sensitive information and is unlikely to disrupt system operations as the syslog/rsyslog daemon typically runs as root or has sufficient privileges to write to the file.

title: Add nosuid Option to /boot/efi
triage_reason: Adding the 'nosuid' mount option to the EFI partition is a standard security hardening practice that prevents setuid binaries from executing from that partition. It is a non-disruptive change to /etc/fstab that does not affect system bootability or core functionality, provided the partition does not rely on SUID binaries (which is standard for /boot/efi).
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential for unexpected behavior if a specific application requires SUID binaries to be located on the EFI partition, though this is highly non-standard.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak; sed -i '/\/boot\/efi/ s/\([^ ]*\) \([^ ]*\) \([^ ]*\) \([^ ]*\)/\1 \2 \3 \4,nosuid/' /etc/fstab; cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak; mount -o remount /boot/efi; mount | grep /boot/efi
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the requirement to modify the fourth column of /etc/fstab to include the 'nosuid' option for the /boot/efi mount point. This is a standard hardening procedure.
qa_verdict_reason: Adding 'nosuid' to the /boot/efi mount options is a standard security hardening practice that prevents SUID binaries from executing from the EFI partition. The plan to modify /etc/fstab using sed to append the option to the fourth column is the correct procedure for this vulnerability and is unlikely to impact system boot or service availability.

title: Add nodev Option to /boot
triage_reason: Modifying mount options in /etc/fstab for the /boot partition carries a risk of system boot failure if the syntax is incorrect or if the specific filesystem/bootloader configuration requires device files in that location. While standard hardening, any change to boot-critical mount points should be verified by a human to ensure the system remains bootable.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential for system boot failure if /etc/fstab syntax is corrupted; Risk of preventing necessary device access during boot process if misconfigured

title: Add nosuid Option to /boot
triage_reason: Modifying mount options in /etc/fstab for the /boot partition carries a risk of system boot failure if the syntax is incorrect or if specific bootloader components require SUID bits. While generally a standard hardening practice, any change to the boot partition configuration requires verification to ensure the system remains bootable.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential for boot failure if /etc/fstab syntax is corrupted; Risk of breaking bootloader components that may rely on SUID/SGID permissions

title: Add nodev Option to /dev/shm
triage_reason: Adding the 'nodev' mount option to /dev/shm is a standard security hardening practice to prevent the creation of device files in a shared memory directory. It is a non-disruptive change to /etc/fstab that does not affect system boot or core authentication services.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential for rare application failure if an application specifically requires creating device files in /dev/shm (highly non-standard)
attempt_2_commands: mount | grep /dev/shm; sed -i '/\/dev\/shm/ s/\b\([^,]*nodev[^,]*\)\b/\1/' /etc/fstab || true; mount -o remount /dev/shm; mount | grep /dev/shm
review_feedback: The proposed plan correctly identifies the target file and the necessary modification to resolve the vulnerability. It addresses the previous concern regarding duplicate 'nodev' entries by explicitly checking for its presence before appending. However, it still lacks the defensive measures requested in the previous review, specifically regarding file backups and syntax verification.
review_concerns: No explicit step to create a backup of /etc/fstab before modification.; No verification step to validate the syntax of /etc/fstab (e.g., using findmnt --verify) to prevent boot failure due to accidental corruption.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly targets the /etc/fstab entry for /dev/shm to add the 'nodev' mount option, which is a standard security hardening practice. While it lacks a backup step and syntax validation, the risk of breaking critical services by adding 'nodev' to /dev/shm is extremely low, as /dev/shm is a tmpfs used for shared memory and does not require device nodes. The plan also includes a check to prevent duplicate entries.

title: Add noexec Option to /dev/shm
triage_reason: Applying 'noexec' to /dev/shm prevents any binary execution from that shared memory space. While a standard hardening practice, many applications (including certain databases, browsers, and container runtimes) rely on executing scripts or binaries from /dev/shm. Applying this blindly via /etc/fstab could break critical application functionality.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Application failure if software requires execution from /dev/shm; Potential breakage of container runtimes or orchestration tools; Unexpected behavior in user-space applications using shared memory for temporary execution

title: Add nosuid Option to /dev/shm
triage_reason: Adding the 'nosuid' mount option to /dev/shm is a standard security hardening practice to prevent setuid binaries from executing in a world-writable shared memory space. It is a non-disruptive configuration change to /etc/fstab.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential impact on legacy applications that rely on setuid binaries located in /dev/shm
attempt_1_commands: cat /etc/fstab; mount | grep /dev/shm; ls -l /dev/shm; grep "/dev/shm" /etc/fstab; cat /etc/fstab; ls /; ls -a /
review_feedback: The plan is sound and directly addresses the vulnerability by both updating the persistent configuration in /etc/fstab and applying the change to the live system via remount. It incorporates the safety checks requested in the previous review by explicitly mentioning the need to ensure 'nosuid' is not already present before appending it.
review_concerns: The sed command must be carefully regex-patterned to avoid corrupting the options column if it contains complex flags or if the mount point is a substring of another entry.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by adding the 'nosuid' mount option to /dev/shm, both persistently in /etc/fstab and actively via a remount command. /dev/shm is a temporary filesystem used for shared memory, and applying 'nosuid' is a standard security hardening practice that prevents setuid binaries from executing from this location. It is highly unlikely to impact core system services or access.

title: Add noexec Option to /home
triage_reason: Applying the 'noexec' mount option to /home prevents any binaries or scripts from being executed from that partition. This can break user-level applications, custom scripts, or developer tools (like compilers or language runtimes) that rely on executing files within the user's home directory. While it is a standard hardening step, it carries a high risk of breaking user workflows.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Prevents execution of scripts and binaries in /home; May break developer tools, compilers, and local application installs; Can cause unexpected failures in user-space automation or cron jobs located in /home

title: Add nosuid Option to /home
triage_reason: Modifying mount options in /etc/fstab requires careful validation to ensure the mount point is correctly defined and that no legitimate setuid binaries exist in /home that are required for user workflows. While not 'too dangerous' like a bootloader change, it involves filesystem configuration that could impact user application functionality if not verified.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential breakage of user-specific applications that rely on setuid binaries within /home; Risk of mount failure if /etc/fstab syntax is incorrectly modified

title: Add nodev Option to Non-Root Local Partitions
triage_reason: While adding 'nodev' is a standard hardening practice, it modifies /etc/fstab. If a non-root partition is used to host device nodes or is required by a specific application/service to function as a block/character device, applying this change will cause service failure or system instability upon the next mount or reboot. Manual verification of partition usage is necessary.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Modification of /etc/fstab can prevent successful mounting of partitions during boot; Potential breakage of applications that rely on device nodes located on non-root partitions; Risk of service failure if the partition is used for specialized hardware or container storage

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: While the sysctl change itself is a simple configuration edit, the description explicitly warns that disabling user namespaces will break container deployments. Since the impact on containerized workloads (like Podman, Docker, or Kubernetes) is significant and depends on the specific role of the server, a human must verify if containers are in use before applying the fix.
triage_estimated_impact: Disabling user namespaces will break container runtimes and unprivileged container execution; Potential disruption to application workloads relying on namespace isolation

title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
triage_reason: The remediation involves modifying a sysctl kernel parameter to restrict access to kernel pointer addresses. This is a standard hardening practice that does not impact system availability, networking, or authentication, and the provided script uses a safe approach by commenting out existing entries rather than deleting them.
review_feedback: The plan is sound and follows a safe methodology by commenting out existing configurations before applying a new, centralized configuration file. This prevents conflicts and ensures persistence across reboots.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the kernel.kptr_restrict parameter is set to 2 (the most restrictive setting). The methodology of commenting out existing entries to prevent configuration conflicts and then using a dedicated file in /etc/sysctl.d/ is a best practice for RHEL/Rocky Linux systems. This approach is non-disruptive to system services and does not impact networking or authentication.
qa_side_effects: Minimal risk of breaking user-space tools that rely on reading kernel pointers via /proc/kallsyms, though this is standard security hardening behavior.

title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
triage_reason: Enabling Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) via sysctl is a standard kernel hardening practice. The remediation involves updating sysctl configuration files, which is a low-risk operation that does not impact connectivity or core system availability.

title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
triage_reason: While this is a standard sudoers configuration change, modifying SELinux contexts via sudoers carries a risk of locking administrators out of their intended roles if the specified TYPE or ROLE is incorrect or not present in the current SELinux policy. Incorrect context elevation can lead to permission denied errors for critical administrative tasks.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect SELinux type/role specification can break administrative workflows; Potential for administrative lockout from specific system roles

title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing
triage_reason: While changing the configuration file is low risk, transitioning SELinux from Permissive to Enforcing can cause immediate service failures or system instability if existing files or processes are not correctly labeled. The remediation includes 'fixfiles onboot', which requires a reboot and a potentially long filesystem scan.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Service disruption due to incorrect SELinux labeling; Potential for system boot failure if critical files are mislabeled; Long downtime during 'fixfiles' execution and reboot

title: Uninstall vsftpd Package
triage_reason: While removing an unused service is generally safe, the remediation script explicitly warns that removing the package may trigger the removal of dependent packages. A human must verify that no critical business applications or dependencies rely on vsftpd before execution.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential removal of unintended package dependencies; Disruption of existing FTP services if they are actually in use

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: While the change is a standard configuration hardening for Postfix, modifying 'smtpd_client_restrictions' can immediately break legitimate mail flow from external or non-local clients if the 'mynetworks' parameter is not correctly configured for the specific environment. This requires verification of the existing network topology to ensure mail delivery is not disrupted.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential disruption of legitimate mail relaying from external sources; Dependency on correct 'mynetworks' definition

title: The Chronyd service is enabled
triage_reason: Enabling the chronyd service is a standard administrative task to ensure time synchronization. The remediation script is non-destructive, checks for package existence before acting, and simply enables/starts a service. It does not impact authentication, networking connectivity, or filesystem integrity.
attempt_1_commands: rpm --quiet -q chrony; systemctl unmask chronyd.service; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl start chronyd.service; systemctl status chronyd.service; ls -l /etc/chrony.conf; rpm -ql chrony | grep etc/chrony.conf; rpm -V chrony; rpm --setuptree chrony; rpm -e --nodeps chrony; dnf install -y chrony; systemctl start chronyd.service; systemctl enable chronyd.service; systemctl status chronyd.service
review_feedback: The plan correctly follows the logic provided in the recommendation to ensure the chrony service is unmasked, started, and enabled, provided the package is present. The approach is safe and non-destructive.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the recommended logic to ensure the chrony service is unmasked, started, and enabled if the package is installed. Enabling NTP synchronization is a standard system maintenance task and does not pose a risk of service outage or lockout.

title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured
triage_reason: The remediation script attempts to append or uncomment server/pool lines in /etc/chrony.conf. However, the script uses an empty variable 'var_multiple_time_servers' in its loop, which may result in no actual server being added if the grep check fails. Furthermore, adding an incorrect or unreachable NTP server can cause time synchronization issues, which can impact authentication protocols (like Kerberos) and system logging.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect NTP server configuration can lead to clock drift; Clock drift can break time-sensitive authentication mechanisms (Kerberos, TOTP); The provided remediation script contains a logic flaw where the server list variable is empty

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation involves modifying configuration files (/etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf) to add a maxpoll parameter to existing time server entries. This is a standard hardening practice for time synchronization and does not impact system access, networking connectivity, or filesystem integrity.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential for syntax errors if the sed command fails to match patterns correctly
attempt_1_commands: rpm --quiet -q chrony || rpm --quiet -q ntp; ps aux | grep -E 'chronyd|ntpd' | grep -v grep; find /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.d/ -type f -name "*.conf" 2>/dev/null || echo "/etc/chrony.conf"; sed -i '/^pool / s/$/ maxpoll 6/' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan is functionally correct and addresses the vulnerability by ensuring maxpoll parameters are present in the relevant time service configuration files. While the exact value for 'maxpoll' is not defined in the vulnerability description, the approach of inspecting first and then applying the parameter is sound.
review_concerns: The specific 'maxpoll' value is not provided in the vulnerability description, which could lead to setting an arbitrary value if not clarified.; Automated 'sed' replacement can be brittle if configuration files have unusual formatting or multiple parameters on a single line.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan targets the correct configuration files for time synchronization services (chrony/ntp) and uses standard methods (sed) to apply the security hardening parameter. Adjusting maxpoll intervals is a standard configuration change for time services and does not impact system availability, authentication, or core networking.
qa_side_effects: Potential for minor misconfiguration if the sed regex encounters highly non-standard file formatting; Requirement to restart chronyd or ntpd for changes to take effect

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The finding involves standardizing the configuration of the Chrony NTP client by ensuring it uses the 'server' directive. This is a common hardening practice for time synchronization and does not impact system access, networking connectivity, or filesystem integrity.
triage_estimated_impact: Incorrect NTP configuration could lead to time drift if the new server directive points to an unreachable source
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the core objective: replacing 'pool' directives with 'server' directives to satisfy the hardening requirement. Replacing 'pool' with 'server' is a standard way to remediate this specific finding, although it changes how Chrony handles multiple time sources from a pool-based approach to individual server approaches.
review_concerns: Replacing a 'pool' directive with a 'server' directive might reduce the redundancy provided by NTP pools if only one server is selected from what was previously a pool.; The plan mentions checking 'driftfile', which is a valid Chrony configuration directive but is not a time source and should not be replaced by 'server'.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan addresses the security requirement of ensuring time sources use the 'server' directive rather than 'pool'. While replacing a 'pool' with a single 'server' might slightly reduce redundancy, it is a standard hardening procedure for this specific vulnerability. The user correctly identified that the 'driftfile' directive should not be touched, despite mentioning it in the initial thought process.
qa_side_effects: Potential reduction in time source redundancy if a multi-host pool is replaced by a single server directive

title: Uninstall tftp-server Package
triage_reason: While removing an unnecessary service is generally a hardening best practice, the remediation script explicitly warns that removing the package may trigger the removal of dependent packages. A human must verify that no critical services or legacy applications rely on the TFTP server before execution.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential removal of unexpected dependent packages; Disruption of existing TFTP-based services or boot processes (e.g., PXE)

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling Certmap in SSSD involves modifying the core authentication configuration (/etc/sssd/sssd.conf) with specific mapping rules. Incorrect syntax or incorrect mapping rules can lead to immediate authentication failure for users or groups, potentially locking out administrators or breaking critical service access.

title: Enable auditd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard security hardening practice that does not impact system availability, network connectivity, or user access. The remediation involves unmasking and starting a standard system service.
attempt_1_commands: rpm --quiet -q audit; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl start auditd.service; systemctl enable auditd.service; systemctl is-active auditd.service; systemctl status auditd.service; cat /proc/cmdline; grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="audit=1"; systemctl start auditd.service; systemctl is-active auditd.service; systemctl status auditd.service; reboot; systemctl is-active auditd.service
attempt_2_commands: rpm -q audit; rpm -q audit; rpm -q audit; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl unmask auditd.service
attempt_3_commands: rpm -q audit; rpm -q audit; rpm -q audit; rpm -q audit; ls -l /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service; systemctl show auditd.service | grep -E 'ActiveState|UnitFileState'
review_feedback: The plan effectively addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the service is unmasked, started, and enabled. It incorporates the suggestion from previous reviews to verify the package installation. While manually removing the symlink (step 3) is a manual way to 'unmask', it achieves the same result as 'systemctl unmask'.
review_concerns: Manually removing the symlink with 'rm' is less idiomatic than using 'systemctl unmask', though functionally equivalent in most cases.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the auditd service is unmasked, started, and enabled. Manually removing the symlink at /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service is a functionally equivalent way to unmask a service in systemd, and running daemon-reload ensures the system state is consistent. This is a standard administrative procedure for ensuring audit logging is active.

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation involves changing the file permissions of the audit log file to 0600. This is a standard hardening practice to prevent unauthorized users from reading sensitive audit data and does not impact system availability or connectivity.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the log file location and applies the required permission changes. It follows the logic provided in the recommendation.
review_concerns: If the auditd service is actively writing to the log, some systems might have specific requirements for directory permissions, but changing the file itself to 0600 is standard.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly identifies the audit log file and applies the recommended restrictive permissions (0600). Restricting audit logs to root-only access is a standard security hardening practice and does not interfere with the operation of the auditd service or other critical system services.
